none: A piece of white lace fabric is laid over purple fabric.

body.creator: g
body.value: Bobbin lace, also known as pillow lace, cushion lace, and bone lace, among other terms, is produced from a “modified weaving process which takes its name from the way it is made – with bobbins, usually of bone or wood, on a pillow or cushion. Basically, the pillow is for sticking pins into. If the pillow is cylindrical it is supported on a stand to raise it to the right height and stop it rolling around. A pattern for the lace is needed, and this is called a pricking because holes are pricked with a sharp needle in a parchment or stiff card. The accurate placing of these holes is extremely important since through them, as the work proceeds, will be passed the pins which hold the threads which are attached to the bobbins which the worker moves to cross or twist the threads to build up the structure of the lace. Therefore everything hangs on the pins, and if they are badly placed, or if the pricking is ‘worn’, i.e. the pin holes have stretched or even run together with too much use, the design of the lace will be distorted... The origin of bobbin lacemaking lies in weaving, which goes back itself many thousands of years... evidence places bobbin lace in Flanders, Switzerland, Germany, Venice, and England in the sixteenth century” (Earnshaw, A Dictionary of Lace, 20). Given its intricacies and rich history, it is no surprise, therefore, that Reynolds would greatly appreciate bobbin lace and plan to dress his new muse with it.

body.creator: g
body.value: In this shot, the layers of lace and satin fill the frame of the shot—the surfaces of the screen and the textile are collapsed, bringing the materiality of the latter to the fore. While the underlayer of the mauve satin retains a sense of dimension, shadows indicating an undulating quality, the center of the lace conveys flatness, bringing the eye to the intricate entanglement of webbing and floral motifs. The shot also prioritizes contrast. Including the finished edge of the lace at the edge of the frame, it suggests that the satin layer is encased within the top layer, visible only due to the porousness of the lace. That is, the underlayer of mauve satin is necessary to make the delicate pattern of the lace visible—it is only through juxtaposition and textural difference that the formal qualities of the garment emerge.
